epigraph.text: My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.
epigraph.quote: When Taylor series fail at singularities, Laurent series prevail.

content.text:
Taylor series represent functions analytic at a point. For functions 
with singularities, we need Laurent series, which include negative powers.

content.title: Laurent's Theorem
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic on an annulus $A = \{z : r < |z - z_0| < R\}$ 
(where $0 \leq r < R \leq \infty$). Then for all $z \in A$:
\[
 f(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n(z - z_0)^n 
 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(z - z_0)^n + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{a_{-n}}{(z - z_0)^n},
\]
where
\[
 a_n = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_\gamma \frac{f(w)}{(w - z_0)^{n+1}}\,dw
\]
for any simple closed contour $\gamma$ in $A$ encircling $z_0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The sum $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_{-n}/(z-z_0)^n$ is called the 
**principal part** of the Laurent series. 
It captures the behavior near the singularity at $z_0$.

content.problem:
Find the Laurent series for $f(z) = \dfrac{1}{z(z-1)}$ in the annulus 
$0 < |z| < 1$.
content.solution:
By partial fractions: $\dfrac{1}{z(z-1)} = \dfrac{-1}{z} + \dfrac{1}{z-1}$.

For $|z| < 1$:
\[
 \frac{1}{z-1} = \frac{-1}{1-z} = -\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} z^n.
\]
Thus
\[
 f(z) = -\frac{1}{z} - 1 - z - z^2 - z^3 - \cdots = -\frac{1}{z} - \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} z^n.
\]
The principal part is $-1/z$; the coefficients are $a_{-1} = -1$, 
$a_n = -1$ for $n \geq 0$.

content.problem: Find the Laurent series for the same function in $|z| > 1$.
content.solution:
Now $|1/z| < 1$, so we expand differently:
\[
 \frac{1}{z-1} = \frac{1}{z} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 1/z} = \frac{1}{z}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{z^n} 
 = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{z^n}.
\]
Thus
\[
 f(z) = -\frac{1}{z} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{z^n} = \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{z^n}.
\]
No principal part for the point at infinity!

content.title: Uniqueness
content.content: The Laurent series of $f$ in an annulus is unique.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This means that however we compute the series—by direct expansion, 
partial fractions, or other methods—the result is the same.

marginNotes.text:
I am the Alpha and the Omega, the 
first and the last, the beginning and the end.

marginNotes.author: Pierre Alphonse Laurent (1843)
marginNotes.text:
The extension of Taylor's 
theorem to include negative powers.
